READ MORE: Top Skechers sneaker deals on Amazon — starting at just $35

On Cloudnova 2 shoes have a unique design that blends comfort with a modern silhouette. As Sharkey explains, "this is a great lifestyle shoe. [It] can be a great choice for anyone that suffers from tight Achilles tendon/calf muscles and plantar fasciitis, as the 14mm heel-to-toe drop helps to accommodate for limited ankle joint range of motion."

If you need another affordable option, Stoke Shoes come in a huge range of colors and wide sizes, all for just $80. Dr. Graham Reid, DPM, AACFAS, FASO FELLOW, specifically recommends these shoes for plantar fasciitis. "Stoke Shoes are designed around many of the features I recommend for patients with heel pain. Their supportive arch geometry, stable platform, cushioned midsole and overall focus on proper foot alignment help reduce the mechanical stress placed on the plantar fascia during walking."

For more deals, visit www.foxnews.com/deals

Original price: $139.95

Foot specialists frequently recommend the Brooks Adrenaline GTS 24 — currently at a 30-day low on Amazon. "This is a great choice for those who need a bit more stability. It offers good cushioning, and the GuideRails support system helps limit excess motion that can put stress on the plantar fascia, making it a solid option for walking, running or all-day wear," says Dr. Gregory Alvarez, DPM, FACFAS, at Ankle & Foot Centers of America.

If you're an Amazon Prime member, you can get some of these shoes sent to your door ASAP. You can join or start a 30-day free trial to start your shopping today.

item.title: Ella Langley suffers wardrobe malfunction while opening for Morgan Wallen in Baltimore
item.description: Videos show Ella Langley's dress unzipping on stage during Morgan Wallen's tour before she grabbed a Baltimore Ravens jersey and kept performing.
item.content:encoded:
Ella Langley isn't stuck in the past or worried about tomorrow after suffering a wardrobe malfunction on stage.

The "Be Her" singer kept her cool after her entire dress became unzipped just minutes into her opening set for Morgan Wallen on his "Still the Problem" tour over the weekend.

Langley, 27, handled the mishap with ease and slipped into a Baltimore Ravens jersey before picking up her guitar and carrying on with the show at M&T Bank Stadium.

MORGAN WALLEN POKES FUN AT ELLA LANGLEY’S ONSTAGE MIX-UP, THEN REPEATS IT

The "Choosing Texas" singer was dressed to impress as she took the stage wearing her signature sparkling pink gown covered in fringe.

In videos circulating online, Langley eagerly covered up in the purple football jersey as rain poured down on the stage.

The country star addressed the incident with her social media followers on Sunday.

ELLA LANGLEY TURNS 63-YEAR-OLD FORMER NFL HEAD COACH INTO 'ELLA FELLA'

"Yesterday, my entire dress came unzipped on song #2 in a 60-minute set in the pouring down rain in a stadium full of people," Langley wrote on Instagram stories with the Stone Temple Pilots song "Wonderful" playing in the background.

Former Ravens defensive coordinator Rex Ryan declared his love for the country star on social media, and admitted he was officially part of the "Ella Fella" fan club.

CLICK HERE TO SIGN UP FOR THE ENTERTAINMENT NEWSLETTER

"Look, I've been around football my whole life. I've coached in Baltimore, won a lot of games, and seen some incredible performances But let me tell you something…Ella Langley had Baltimore in the palm of her hand tonight. As the kids say, I'm officially an Ella Fella. I don't make the rules," he wrote on X.

Langley didn't let the wardrobe snafu ruin her weekend or her continued rise in super stardom.

LIKE WHAT YOU'RE READING? CLICK HERE FOR MORE ENTERTAINMENT NEWS

Her "Choosing Texas" anthem placed at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 charts, marking not only its 14th week at the top, but also tying a record with two iconic hits.

The country ballad first topped the Hot 100 in February, and now matches Mariah Carey's "We Belong Together" and Whitney Houston's "I Will Always Love You" for most weeks at No. 1 among non-holiday titles by women with no male-billed acts.

In addition, "Choosing Texas" ties Carey's "All I Want for Christmas Is You" for second-longest ranking hit.

item.title: ESPN parting ways with NFL analyst Ryan Clark ahead of network's first Super Bowl broadcast
item.description: Ryan Clark is among the latest ESPN layoffs, as the sports analyst departs the network in a move that signals continued shifts in sports media.
item.content:encoded:
ESPN is parting ways with NFL analyst Ryan Clark, multiple sources told OutKick.

Clark is expected to be part of a round of layoffs the network could announce as soon as Tuesday.

The NFL analyst will be among the highest-paid on-air personalities ESPN cuts, sources said, earning roughly $2 million per year. It is unclear whether Clark’s contract is expiring or whether he is being sidelined with time remaining on his deal.

Clark joined ESPN after retiring from the NFL in 2015 and re-signed with the network in 2021 for three years. In 2024, the two sides had contentious contract negotiations and Clark posted on social media at the time that he felt he "deserved more" and ESPN "disagreed." Ultimately, the two sides agreed to a deal.

ESPN RE-SIGNS FAR-LEFT, ANTI-SYDNEY SWEENEY RACE-BAITER WHILE REPORTEDLY CONSIDERING MASS LAYOFFS

The timing of the decision is particularly notable given Clark's role on ESPN's Monday Night Countdown pregame NFL coverage. The network is set to carry its first Super Bowl on February 14. Sources say ESPN is considering both internal and external candidates to replace Clark on its Super Bowl studio coverage.

Clark has become an increasingly polarizing figure inside ESPN in recent years. Sage Steele alleged in her lawsuit against the network that Clark refused to work with her on-air.

CLICK HERE FOR MORE OUTKICK SPORTS COVERAGE

Over the past year, he has also generated headlines for criticizing Robert Griffin III over his marriage to a White woman, an on-air exchange with Peter Schrager that reportedly continued off-air, and making disputed claims about how Louisiana police officers handled the fatal crash investigation involving former LSU player Kyren Lacy, who died by suicide.

If you or someone you know is having thoughts of suicide, please contact the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-TALK (8255).

OutKick reached out to ESPN for comment on Clark’s status with the network, but the network did not provide a statement before publication.

This is a breaking news story. Check back with OutKick for updates.

item.title: One six-pack of beer could significantly alter how the Constitution regulates law enforcement
item.description: Kenneth Jouppi's $95,000 Cessna plane was seized over a six-pack of beer, and now the Supreme Court will decide if it violates the 8th Amendment.
item.content:encoded:
The Supreme Court agreed Monday to hear an Alaska man’s challenge to the state’s effort to confiscate his plane over a six-pack of beer, setting the stage for a case that could upend the ability of law enforcement to impose asset forfeiture.

Kenneth Jouppi, now 82, was operating a one-man air taxi service using his personally-owned 1969 Cessna plane in 2012 when Alaska state troopers executed a search warrant on his plane and found alcohol on board, including one six-pack that they said was plainly visible. At the time, Jouppi was on a runway in Fairbanks preparing to take a customer and her groceries to the remote village of Beaver near the center of the state.

Having beer on board wasn’t illegal on its own. However, since Beaver had banned the possession or sale of alcohol within its limits, Jouppi was convicted of alcohol importation, a misdemeanor. The Alaska Supreme Court held that, under the alcohol importation ban, the state was required to confiscate Jouppi’s Cessna.

GOP LAWMAKERS URGE SUPREME COURT TO STRENGTHEN FIRST AMENDMENT PROTECTIONS FOR STREET PREACHERS

Jouppi has since argued in court that the state’s seizure of his plane, valued at $95,000, runs afoul of the 8th Amendment’s ban on excessive fines. Now, the Supreme Court will weigh in on whether he’s right.

When ruling on Jouppi’s case, the court will have the opportunity to seriously rein in the ability of state and local governments to impose asset forfeiture — a longtime goal of civil libertarians.

"For centuries, English common law took into account the characteristics of the defendant and his or her specific violation in determining excessiveness," a trio of scholars working for the libertarian Cato Institute wrote of the case. "When courts lose touch with the original understanding of a fundamental right, they risk watering down the constitutional protections that must be afforded to everyday people. Such dilution of rights is especially dangerous today as governments generate billions of dollars in revenue through civil and criminal asset forfeiture."

SUPREME COURT UNANIMOUSLY LIMITS USE OF GUN LAW USED TO PROSECUTE HUNTER BIDEN

"This case gives the Supreme Court the opportunity to ensure that the people are afforded the protection that the Eighth Amendment promises them," they added.

Alaska, however, argues that confiscating the planes of those who facilitate illegal imports is the only way the state can meaningfully prevent the problems caused by alcoholism in its remote regions.

DOJ SUES DENVER OVER BAN ON ‘ASSAULT WEAPONS’ AS CITY'S DEM MAYOR SAYS IT 'WILL NOT BE BULLIED'

"Alcohol abuse is a serious problem in rural Alaska, and airplanes are sometimes the only realistic means to commit the crime of alcohol importation," a legal brief from the state reads. "Jouppi was convicted for his own knowing and intentional misconduct, and forfeiture removes the business asset that was the means used to commit the offense."

Some villages in Alaska are not connected to major road networks, making them primarily accessible by small planes, such as the one owned by Jouppi.

A trial court in 2018 ruled that the state taking Jouppi’s plane would be an excessive fine under the Constitution, though the Alaska Supreme Court ultimately decided otherwise in 2025, leaving the United States Supreme Court as the one-time pilot’s only remaining outlet for recourse.

Oral arguments in Jouppi’s case are likely to take place either later this year or early next year, with a decision expected to come down by next summer.

item.title: Gunman charged after wounding 9 in Tucson nightlife shooting before cop takes him down
item.description: A 21-year-old suspect faces multiple felony charges after allegedly opening fire in Tucson’s downtown nightlife district, wounding nine people before police took him down.
item.content:encoded:
A 21-year-old man is facing a slew of charges after allegedly opening fire in a packed downtown Tucson nightlife district early Sunday, wounding nine people before police took him down.

David Leroy French was charged with nine counts of aggravated assault with a deadly weapon, nine counts of aggravated assault causing serious physical injury and one count of discharging a firearm at a structure, a Tucson Police Department spokesperson told Fox News Digital.

French was booked in absentia while hospitalized and will be taken to the Pima County Adult Detention Complex after his release, police said.

TUCSON MASS SHOOTING HAS RESIDENTS CONCERNED OVER ONGOING RAMPANT VIOLENCE, POLS DEBATING GUN RIGHTS

Additional charges are pending, the spokesperson added.

The shooting erupted around 2 a.m. in the 100 block of East Congress Street, a busy area lined with bars, restaurants and nightclubs.

An interim complaint said the gunfire followed a verbal altercation. Investigators identified French as the shooter after reviewing surveillance footage, according to the Arizona Daily Star.

Officers patrolling nearby heard the shots and rushed to the scene on foot, police said.

MASS SHOOTING ROCKS CROWDED DOWNTOWN TUCSON DISTRICT, LEAVING 10 WOUNDED

Police said officers saw the suspect running from the area and repeatedly ordered him to stop. 

After he allegedly ignored their commands, a uniformed officer fired his weapon and struck him.

The suspect was taken into custody and given first aid before being transported to a hospital in life-threatening condition.

TEEN BRAWL INSIDE IN-N-OUT SENDS CUSTOMERS RUNNING DURING MONSOON STORM, VIDEO SHOWS

Nine other people were shot and taken to local hospitals.

Police said most were wounded in the arms or legs. No deaths were reported, and no officers were injured.

The Pima County Sheriff’s Department told Fox News Digital that it is not releasing French’s mugshot at this time, citing a ruling by the 9th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals.

Fox News Digital's Bonny Chu contributed to this report.

item.title: Catholic nuns sue New York, say assisted suicide law forces them to violate their faith
item.description: Catholic nuns who have cared for 45,000 dying patients sue New York, claiming the Medical Aid in Dying Act forces them to facilitate assisted suicide.
item.content:encoded:
Catholic nuns and faith-based healthcare providers who have spent generations caring for New York's dying are suing the state, arguing its new assisted suicide law will force them to violate their religious beliefs by participating in a practice they consider morally wrong.

The lawsuit, filed Friday in federal court by over 10 Catholic plaintiffs, including the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, the Little Sisters of the Poor, the Diocese of Rockville Centre and Catholic Health, argues New York's Medical Aid in Dying Act compels religious healthcare providers to counsel terminally ill patients about physician-assisted suicide, make referrals to participating providers and, in some cases, accommodate assisted suicide in their facilities.

The plaintiffs say those requirements violate the First Amendment, federal healthcare protections and longstanding Catholic teaching on the sanctity of human life.

REP CLAUDIA TENNEY: WHY IS NEW YORK PUNISHING NUNS WHO CARE FOR THE DYING?

"New York has put the Sisters to a stark choice: either abandon their religious beliefs regarding the sanctity of life or face significant fines and penalties," the lawsuit reads. "Through its euphemistically-named Medical Aid in Dying Act, which builds upon requirements in the Palliative Care Information Act, New York has conscripted even religious healthcare providers and institutions to participate in the provision of physician-assisted suicide."

New York’s Medical Aid in Dying Act, which was signed into law in February, lets those who are 18 years and older who have been diagnosed with a terminal illness and given six months or less to live to request and self-administer life-ending medication if they are experiencing immense suffering, according to Compassion and Choices, who supports the new law.

The group notes the measure is based on Oregon's long-standing assisted dying law.

HOSPICE CEO ASKS CONGRESS HOW A PROVIDER CAN OPERATE 'OUT OF A BURRITO STAND IN CALIFORNIA' WITH NO OVERSIGHT

According to the complaint, the law could compel religious organizations to facilitate physician-assisted suicide by giving patients information about the practice, permitting staff to discuss it with terminally ill patients, and helping patients complete steps required to qualify for life-ending medication.

The plaintiffs also argue the law would require referrals to willing providers and prevent the organizations from disciplining employees who provide such counseling or assistance in conflict with the institutions' religious beliefs.

Rosary Hill Home, run by the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, one of the plaintiffs, has cared for roughly 45,000 low-income cancer patients in New York, providing free end-of-life care regardless of their faith or cultural background.

SUPREME COURT UNANIMOUSLY SLAPS DOWN BLUE STATE TARGETING PRO-LIFE GROUP

Mother Marie Edward, O.P., Superior General of the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, said, "For over 125 years, we have ministered to the poor who are dying of cancer as if they were Christ Himself. Our calling is to offer comfort, prayer, and loving medical care to those in their final days—not death."

Edward added, "We pray the court will protect our freedom to remain faithful to that ministry God has entrusted to us."

In April, the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne sued New York Gov. Kathy Hochul, alleging the state’s "Lesbian, gay, bisexual and transgender, and people living with HIV long-term care facility residents' bill of rights" could force them to choose between their faith and their mission or face fines, loss of licensing and even jail time.

Mother Mary Rose Heery, O. Carm., prioress general of the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, said in a statement, "At our homes, we bring Christ’s unbounded love to elderly New Yorkers of every background and walk of life."

Heery added, "We strive to bring His compassion to those entrusted to our care, ensuring that no resident ever has to die alone. This law strikes at the heart of that vocation."

Mark Rienzi, president of Becket, who is representing the Carmelite Sisters, the Dominican Sisters, the Benedictine Sisters, and the Little Sisters of the Poor, as well as Bishop John O. Barres and the Diocese of Rockville Centre, said in a statement that, "New York is forcing sick and dying people to consider suicide at their lowest moment, regardless of whether they ask for it or not."

"Worse, it’s conscripting the nuns who care for them into its suicide cult on pain of crushing fines and professional ruin," Rienzi added. "That is both unconscionable and unconstitutional. We’re confident the federal courts will put a stop to it."

Martin Nussbaum, general counsel for Catholic Benefits Association, who is representing the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne in the transgender bill of rights lawsuit, told Fox News Digital that the Catholic Benefits Association "is entirely supportive of the Becket lawsuit seeking religious exemption from New York’s assisted suicide law."

"Just as in the Catholic Benefits Association case for the Dominican Sisters of Hawthorne, the State of New York is trampling on the religious liberty and speech rights of Catholic nuns providing selfless service to men and women on their final journey," Nussbaum added.

Fox News Digital reached out to Hochul, New York Attorney General Letitia James, the Carmelite Sisters for the Aged and Infirm, the Little Sisters of the Poor, and the Diocese of Rockville Centre and Catholic Health for comment.
